text: To understand the active structure of a catalyst can be very difficult and often even impossible. It is very unlikely that the catalyst structure remains unchanged under catalytic conditions relative to the initial as-synthesized structure. Therefore, an ideal experiment to study a structure of a catalyst would have to be performed in situ. In this paper we used X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) as an in situ tool to study the structure of Pt3Ni nanoframe particles which we had previously identified as an excellent catalyst for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). The surface properties of these nanoframes were probed by means of electrochemical hydrogen underpotential deposition and carbon monoxide electrooxidation experiments and it was found that different structure nanoframes possessed a different strength towards adsorption of molecules. It is now well understood that a Pt-skin is beneficial to the ORR catalyst performance by reducing the adsorbate binding strengths at the surface. Using ex situ and in situ XAS, we demonstrated that those nanoframes which possess a stronger adsorbate binding typically possess a rougher Pt surface with a less complete surface segregation of Pt due to higher Ni dissolution; whereas the nanoframes that possess higher ORR activity also possess a more pronounced surface segregation of Pt over Ni-rich subsurface layers and consequently a better formed of the beneficial Pt-skin surface structure.
